title: The standards move with the answer
scenario: A board member demands airtight proof when a proposal threatens her preferred strategy, but accepts much thinner evidence when a similar proposal protects the strategy she already wants.
moveOptions: Set one written evidence standard before discussing which proposal feels more attractive.
Let each side choose its own standard so the conversation stays flexible.
Trust the proposal that creates the least emotional discomfort.
Stop asking for evidence once one side sounds more morally confident.
correctMove: Set one written evidence standard before discussing which proposal feels more attractive.
biasExplanation: The issue is not only selective search. The evidential bar itself is moving with the preferred conclusion.
moveExplanation: A precommitted standard exposes whether one side is being protected by a friendlier burden of proof.

title: We already spent too much to stop
scenario: A department keeps funding a failing internal tool mainly because cancelling it now would make the last two years of expense and political effort feel humiliating.
moveOptions: Re-evaluate the options from today forward without counting the past investment as a reason to continue.
Protect morale by refusing to discuss the money and time already spent.
Continue until the original budget is fully exhausted so the sacrifice means something.
Judge the tool by how passionately its original sponsors still defend it.
correctMove: Re-evaluate the options from today forward without counting the past investment as a reason to continue.
biasExplanation: Past spending is being treated as a forward-looking justification for further spending.
moveExplanation: The clean repair is to compare current options on future value rather than on symbolic respect for unrecoverable costs.

title: Keep the policy unless the replacement is perfect
scenario: A team treats the current policy as automatically acceptable, while demanding that any proposed replacement clear a much higher evidential bar before it can even be piloted.
moveOptions: Rewrite the existing policy as Option A and compare it against alternatives under the same decision criteria.
Assume the current policy earned its place simply by lasting this long.
Keep the replacement off the table until it can guarantee zero downside.
Judge the current policy by how familiar it feels to the team.
correctMove: Rewrite the existing policy as Option A and compare it against alternatives under the same decision criteria.
biasExplanation: The current arrangement is being spared scrutiny because it is current, not because it has clearly won the comparison.
moveExplanation: Making the existing arrangement explicit as one option among peers removes the invisible privilege continuity was getting.

title: The charming candidate must be strong at everything
scenario: After a candidate gives a poised, warm opening answer, interviewers start inflating their ratings of judgment, technical depth, and reliability even in areas they have barely tested.
moveOptions: Score each dimension separately and require evidence for every dimension before averaging them into an overall rating.
Let the strongest first impression stand because it captures fit more naturally than a rubric.
Ask the most senior interviewer to decide which trait matters most.
Replace the structured interview with a longer informal conversation.
correctMove: Score each dimension separately and require evidence for every dimension before averaging them into an overall rating.
biasExplanation: One favorable cue is spilling over into unrelated judgments that have not earned the same confidence.
moveExplanation: Separating dimensions prevents one positive impression from licensing a whole-person verdict too early.

title: The first-pass screen with visible identity cues
scenario: Managers insist they are evaluating only professionalism, but the first-pass review still includes names, photos, and school signals even though those cues are not needed to judge the work sample.
moveOptions: Hide unnecessary identity cues on the first pass and use explicit scoring criteria for the material being judged.
Keep all the identity cues visible because experienced managers can correct for them mentally.
Replace the first pass with an unstructured gut-feel discussion.
Assume any evaluator who denies bias is already neutral enough.
correctMove: Hide unnecessary identity cues on the first pass and use explicit scoring criteria for the material being judged.
biasExplanation: Unneeded social markers are being allowed to influence a judgment that is supposed to rest on the work itself.
moveExplanation: Masking the cues and tightening the rubric reduces the chance that fast stereotype-linked signals steer the call.

title: One ugly incident defines the whole vendor
scenario: After one painfully visible failure, a client starts describing an entire vendor relationship as a disaster even though the broader service record has been mostly solid and low-drama.
moveOptions: Count the positive, neutral, and negative episodes separately before letting the worst incident own the whole evaluation.
Use the most painful incident as the only serious evidence because bad events are always more informative.
Ignore the failure completely so the review stays balanced.
Judge the relationship by whichever side sounds angrier in the meeting.
correctMove: Count the positive, neutral, and negative episodes separately before letting the worst incident own the whole evaluation.
biasExplanation: The bad incident may matter, but it is receiving more global weight than the total record clearly warrants.
moveExplanation: Restoring the surrounding base line helps determine whether the negative event is diagnostic or simply dominating attention.

title: Learning only from the winners on stage
scenario: A founder draws strategic lessons from ten celebrated startup stories without asking how many similar startups followed the same playbook and disappeared before anyone invited them onstage.
moveOptions: Reconstruct the hidden denominator by asking how many similar cases failed or vanished before treating the visible winners as representative.
Focus on the most inspiring winners because the market has already validated them.
Ignore the failed firms because failure stories are less actionable.
Assume public visibility is a good filter for truth.
correctMove: Reconstruct the hidden denominator by asking how many similar cases failed or vanished before treating the visible winners as representative.
biasExplanation: The visible success stories are being treated as the sample, even though survival is what made them visible in the first place.
moveExplanation: Restoring the missing failures is the only way to know whether the lesson came from success or from selection.

title: Everyone probably agrees with me
scenario: A manager assumes most of the team privately supports a new policy because the people he talks to most often support it and because open disagreement in meetings has been sparse.
moveOptions: Collect anonymous independent responses before treating local conversational ease as evidence of the wider distribution.
Assume silence means agreement because disagreement would have surfaced otherwise.
Poll only the people most engaged in the topic because they care the most.
Use your own preference as the best estimate of what reasonable people probably think.
correctMove: Collect anonymous independent responses before treating local conversational ease as evidence of the wider distribution.
biasExplanation: The manager is overestimating how widely his own view is shared based on a partial and socially distorted sample.
moveExplanation: Anonymous first-pass data is the quickest way to separate local ease from actual consensus.

title: The win proves my talent, the loss proves the environment
scenario: A salesperson explains a strong quarter as proof of skill and discipline, then explains the next weak quarter almost entirely in terms of bad territory, poor timing, and weak leads.
moveOptions: List the strongest internal and external explanations for both outcomes before deciding which factors carried the most weight.
Assume the better quarter reveals the truth and the weaker quarter is too contaminated by circumstances to analyze.
Use the more flattering explanation to protect motivation.
Judge both quarters entirely by whether the salesperson feels confident about them now.
correctMove: List the strongest internal and external explanations for both outcomes before deciding which factors carried the most weight.
biasExplanation: Success and failure are being explained through noticeably different standards in a self-protective direction.
moveExplanation: Forcing both types of causes onto both outcomes is a direct check against ego-driven credit and blame sorting.

title: The famous name at the end of the memo
scenario: Executives begin treating a recommendation as almost settled once they learn a famous advisor endorsed it, even though the written rationale itself is thin and the advisor's expertise is only adjacent to the problem.
moveOptions: Evaluate the recommendation once with the source label hidden and ask what support the reasons themselves actually provide.
Give extra weight to the memo because a famous person would not risk endorsing a weak idea.
Treat adjacent expertise as close enough for any strategic question.
Avoid asking follow-up questions so the advisor's confidence is not undermined.
correctMove: Evaluate the recommendation once with the source label hidden and ask what support the reasons themselves actually provide.
biasExplanation: Prestige is being allowed to substitute for issue-specific support and domain fit.
moveExplanation: Source-blind evaluation helps separate borrowed status from the actual argumentative weight of the memo.

title: I can explain it, so I must understand it
scenario: A student feels certain she understands a public policy after hearing a polished summary, but when asked to explain the mechanism step by step and apply it to a new case, the structure quickly collapses.
moveOptions: Require a step-by-step explanation plus one transfer case before counting the concept as understood.
Trust the feeling of fluency because explanations that come smoothly are usually accurate enough.
Move on once the terminology can be repeated confidently.
Use the same example again until it sounds even cleaner.
correctMove: Require a step-by-step explanation plus one transfer case before counting the concept as understood.
biasExplanation: The learner is mistaking familiarity and fluent recall for deeper structural understanding.
moveExplanation: A mechanism walk-through and transfer test reveal whether the understanding can do real cognitive work or only repeat a smooth surface summary.

title: Leaving the harmful policy untouched feels cleaner
scenario: Leaders know a legacy workflow is producing regular low-level harm, but they still resist changing it because any mistake during the transition would feel more directly traceable to them than the ongoing harm of leaving it alone.
moveOptions: Compare the consequences of action and inaction side by side instead of treating the untouched workflow as morally neutral.
Assume the safer path is to avoid acting because inaction carries less responsibility.
Wait until the legacy workflow causes a dramatic crisis and then decide quickly.
Judge the issue only by whether the transition would produce bad headlines.
correctMove: Compare the consequences of action and inaction side by side instead of treating the untouched workflow as morally neutral.
biasExplanation: Inaction is being granted unwarranted moral innocence even though it actively preserves the harmful outcome.
moveExplanation: Putting both paths on the same consequence table prevents omission from hiding behind the appearance of passivity.
